paragraphs.text: The Internet has long been hailed as a bastion of free expression and open access to information. However, this ideal is increasingly being challenged by the actions of a new class of gatekeepers: the infrastructure providers, payment processors and so on. Gelbooru situation is particularly concerning due to the manner in which the censorship was implemented. Gelbooru had already taken steps to comply with Cloudflare's requests by removing/censoring content that was deemed objectionable. Despite this, months after it, Cloudflare allegedly altered its services in a way to obscure and censor the site without any new notice or clear communication about the specific policy violations.This lack of transparency and due process is a significant red flag. It leaves the website's operators in the dark, unable to understand the precise nature of the offense or what steps they might take to remedy the situation. Such actions erode trust in the service provider and create an environment of arbitrary enforcement. Furthermore, the reported inability to access Gelbooru via a VPN after the Cloudflare action adds another layer of complexity and concern. VPNs are essential tools for online privacy and for bypassing geographical or political censorship.If a CDN provider's actions are so pervasive that they can circumvent these privacy tools, it demonstrates a level of control over internet traffic that is profoundly worrying. It suggests that Cloudflare's enforcement mechanisms are not just targeting the website itself, but are also designed to block users from accessing the site, regardless of their location or attempts to protect their online identity. This blurs the line between protecting a website from harm and actively preventing users from accessing it, effectively becoming a global censor. The core problem with Cloudflare's actions is the conflation of infrastructure and content. When a company that provides fundamental internet services begins to make judgments about the legality or morality of the content it carries, it sets a dangerous precedent. Unlike a social media platform, which can reasonably be expected to moderate the content posted on its own servers to avoid privacy violations, defamation or promotion of illegal activities such as online scam, fraud and so on, a CDN is supposed to be a neutral conduit. It is a utility, much like an electric company or a water provider. We would not accept an electric company shutting off power to a house because it disapproves of the books being read inside; similarly, a company that provides the underlying infrastructure for the web should not be making editorial decisions about the content flowing through its pipes. If a CDN found out about illegal activities, it should report it to the police, but some contents are legal in certain countries and illegal in others, some countries have sharia, others are under dictatorship, others are democrats and so on If the site and the cloud are based on democrats countries, sharia shouldnt be directly applied to them, instead they can be blocked by IP by the interested countries if they really dont want to let their population visit them.Danbooru has been censored like Gelbooru and they are not alone. Also payment processors applied censorship to many other sites, games, and art Several governments censor information as well, such as online news and freedom of speech, like the UK, China and North Korea But also Iran, Vietnam and more countries are engaged in such activities, some of them even spy on citizens. Online privacy is important as well, because less privacy means that you are more likely to have your identity cloned; without privacy you risk more discrimination as a consequence of your personal choice and/or belief. Where our online data are stored is concerning as well We know very little about where our data goes after we forcefully agree on cookies and such things.Preserving online art is important too, check also Stop Killing Games!
